5 min read
Just now

--

Every explanation of neural networks eventually mentions the human brain. “Loosely inspired by biological neurons.” “Mimics how the brain processes information.” “Artificial neurons connected like the ones in your head.” It’s not wrong exactly. The early conceptual inspiration did come from neuroscience in the 1940s. But the brain analogy tends to create confusion more often than clarity, because a modern neural network doesn’t actually work much like a brain at all. It works more like a huge, very systematic chain of math operations. Here’s what’s actually happening, without the biology metaphor.
The Basic Building Block: One Neuron
A single artificial neuron—sometimes called a node or a unit—does three simple things. It takes some input numbers. It multiplies each input by a corresponding weight (a number that starts random and is adjusted during training). It adds up all those weighted inputs, adds one more adjustable number called a bias, and then applies a simple function to the result called an activation function.
The activation function introduces non-linearity into the whole system and matters more than its simple description suggests. Without it, stacking layers of neurons would just be linear algebra, which is mathematically equivalent to having just one layer, no matter how deep the network is. The activation function allows deeper networks to learn curved, complex, non-linear patterns rather than just straight-line relationships.
The most commonly used activation function today is called ReLU, Rectified Linear Unit, and it does something absurdly simple: if the input is less than zero, output zero; otherwise, output the input unchanged. That’s it. That one rule, applied across millions of neurons, turns out to be enough to learn remarkably complex patterns when the network is deep enough and trained on enough data.
Layers: Where “Deep” Learning Comes From
A neural network organizes its neurons into layers. The first layer is the input layer. It simply receives the raw data, whether those are pixel values from an image, numerical features from a spreadsheet, or tokenized words from a sentence. The last layer is the output layer. It produces the prediction, whether that’s a probability, a category, or a continuous value. Everything in between is a hidden layer, and the word “deep” in deep learning just means the network has multiple hidden layers rather than just one.
The depth matters because of what each layer learns to detect. The first hidden layer in an image-processing network might learn to detect simple edges, bright-to-dark transitions in specific directions. The second layer might learn to combine those edges into corners and curves. The third layer might learn to recognize texture patterns. By the time you get to the later layers, the network is detecting concepts. The output looks like a face. The output looks like text. The output has the shape of a stop sign. None of that hierarchy was programmed in. It emerged from training, layer by layer, because each layer was building on the abstractions captured by the layer before it.
This is the part that genuinely earns some awe: a well-trained deep network discovers a hierarchy of representations on its own, from raw input to meaningful concepts, through nothing more than gradient descent applied repeatedly to labeled examples.
What Training Is Actually Doing to the Network
At the start of training, every weight in the network is set randomly. The model makes predictions that are basically nonsense, no better than guessing. Training then runs a loop that repeats potentially millions of times. The model sees a batch of examples and makes predictions. The predictions are compared to the correct answers, and the gap between them, the loss, is calculated. Then a process called backpropagation traces that error backwards through every layer of the network, calculating exactly how much each weight contributed to the mistake. Finally, every weight is nudged slightly in the direction that would have reduced the error, using gradient descent.
Repeat that loop enough times, across enough examples, and the weights slowly settle into values that produce accurate predictions. The model didn’t receive any direct instruction about what features to look for. It discovered them through repeated exposure to examples and through repeated correction of its mistakes.
What Neural Networks Are Actually Good At
The honest answer is that neural networks are remarkable at certain specific types of problems and much less impressive at others. They excel at problems with high-dimensional input images, audio, and text, where the relevant features are hard to specify in advance and must be discovered from data. They struggle with small datasets, where there aren’t enough examples to learn generalizable patterns before overfitting. They produce outputs without explanations, which limits their usefulness in domains where reasoning about a decision is as important as the decision itself.
The mathematical property that underpins all of these issues is called the Universal Approximation Theorem, proved in 1989: a neural network with even a single hidden layer, given enough neurons, can approximate any continuous function to any desired accuracy. In plain language, the architecture is theoretically capable of representing any pattern. The practical question of whether training will actually yield that representation and whether the network will generalize rather than memorize is a separate matter entirely and is where most of the real work lives.
Why “Not Like the Brain” Is Actually More Impressive
There are roughly 86 billion neurons in a human brain, each connected to thousands of others through synapses that process signals using electrochemical mechanisms that took billions of years of evolution to develop. A typical large language model has billions of numerical parameters being adjusted by a relatively simple optimization algorithm running on a computer for a few weeks. They’re not the same thing in any meaningful biological sense, and the brain metaphor can create unrealistic expectations in both directions. Either “these systems are about to be conscious” or “they’re just simple math, nothing impressive.”
The honest middle ground is more interesting: neural networks are a mathematical framework that turned out to be genuinely powerful at learning representations from data, and that power comes not from imitating biology but from the combination of scale, non-linearity, and gradient-based optimization. Understanding that combination is what actually helps you work with these systems effectively.
The Question Worth Sitting With
Here’s something that genuinely matters for anyone building intuitions about AI: neural networks work, at a practical level, much better than they theoretically should. The Universal Approximation Theorem guarantees that they can represent complex functions, but it says nothing about whether gradient descent will find those representations or whether the representations the training finds will generalize. By rights, training a randomly initialized network with a simple gradient update should fail far more often than it succeeds. The loss landscape is high-dimensional and full of potential traps.
And yet, in practice, large neural networks trained on large datasets with modern optimizers converge reliably to useful solutions. Researchers have studied why, and the current best understanding is that the high dimensionality of the parameter space actually helps: in very high dimensions, local minima are rare, and most local minima are close to the global minimum anyway. The very thing that makes neural networks seem theoretically intractable — millions of parameters — turns out to make them more tractable in practice than a simpler model with fewer parameters would be.
That result is counterintuitive and worth knowing, because it’s one of the best examples in machine learning of empirical reality turning out to be more forgiving than theory would predict — and understanding where that gap exists is part of what makes working with these systems genuinely interesting.
This article is adapted from concepts in Machine Learning: The Complete Beginner’s Guide by Md Shafiqul Baten Sumon, available on Amazon.
